claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in approved education
Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

**First, let's start with your child:**

**How old is the child you're claiming for?** 

The age category is important because:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify (subject to other checks)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Young people **aged 20 or over** generally don't qualify

Please tell me:
1. How old is your child?
2. What is your child's name? (This helps me keep track if you have multiple children)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi, thanks for helping me with this. 

I actually have two children I'd like to ask about - Alex and Blake.

Alex is 16, and Blake is 5 years old.

Should I give you information about both of them, or do you want to go through them one at a time?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great that you're checking for both Alex and Blake. I can definitely help you with both children.

It will be most efficient if we **go through them one at a time**, as the eligibility criteria differ based on age and circumstances. Let me assess each child separately to give you a clear answer for both.

Let's start with **Blake (age 5)** since that assessment will be more straightforward, and then we'll move on to **Alex (age 16)** who will need some additional questions about education or training.

---

**Starting with Blake (5 years old):**

Since Blake is under 16, they fall into the category that may automatically qualify. However, I need to check one important thing:

**Is Blake working full-time?** (This would mean working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment)

This is unlikely for a 5-year-old, but I need to ask as part of the official assessment process.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake's only 5 - they're not working at all! They're just at primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected! Since Blake is 5 years old and not working, let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now, the next question for Blake:

**Is Blake receiving any benefits in their own right?** (Such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)

Again, this is very unlikely for a 5-year-old, but I need to confirm as part of the official process.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with Blake's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Blake is only 5 years old and in primary school, they're definitely not in any apprenticeship program.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, let's move on to questions about **you as the claimant**. These questions will apply to both Blake and Alex.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **ANY** of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**, OR
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
3. **You have pre-settled status** and:
   - You're working and earning (or expect to earn) above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months, OR
   - You're actively looking for work (up to 91 days), OR
   - You have sufficient resources to support yourself and family AND you're NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit, OR
   - You're studying and have sufficient resources, OR
   - You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside, OR
4. **You're living abroad as a Crown servant** or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since you live in the UK, you meet the residency requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about your responsibility for the children.

**Are you responsible for Blake (and Alex)?**

You're considered responsible if **ANY** of the following apply:

1. **You live with the child**, OR
2. **You contribute to the child's upkeep** - paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents), OR
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement that equals or exceeds the Child Benefit amount

**Do you live with Blake and Alex, or contribute to their upkeep in another way?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Well, Blake lives with me at home. 

But Alex doesn't live with me - Alex lives with their other parent. However, I do help out financially with Alex's costs - I contribute towards clothes, school things, that sort of stuff.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that! Let me understand the situation correctly:

- **Blake (5 years old)**: Lives with you at home ✓
- **Alex (16 years old)**: Lives with their other parent, but you contribute financially towards their costs

For Blake, you clearly meet the responsibility requirement since you live together.

For Alex, I need to understand a bit more about your financial contributions:

**Do you contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards Alex's upkeep?**

To give you context, the current Child Benefit rates are:
- **Eldest/only child**: £25.60 per week (about £111 per month)
- **Additional children**: £16.95 per week (about £73 per month)

Your contributions can include:
- Money for clothes, school things, activities
- Regular payments for their care
- Pocket money
- Even occasional lump sum payments

**Do your contributions to Alex meet or exceed this amount?** And are these contributions regular?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep, so that's more than the Child Benefit amount. I do this regularly to help with their costs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! That's very helpful. Since you contribute £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, you clearly meet the responsibility requirement for Alex (as this exceeds the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week for an eldest child).

So you meet the responsibility requirement for **both Blake and Alex**.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about other claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake or Alex?**

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. 

For example:
- Is Alex's other parent (who Alex lives with) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?
- Is anyone claiming for Blake?

Please let me know the situation for each child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Blake lives with me, but their other parent actually already claims Child Benefit for Blake.

For Alex, no one else is claiming - it's just me contributing the money towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. This creates two different situations:

**For Blake (5 years old):**
You mentioned Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. This is important because **only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time**.

**Question about Blake:** Are you and Blake's other parent in agreement about who should be claiming? For example:
- Are you happy for them to continue claiming, OR
- Would you like to claim instead and would they agree to transfer the claim to you, OR
- Is there a disagreement about who should claim?

**For Alex (16 years old):**
You said no one else is claiming for Alex, which is good. Since Alex is 16, I'll need to ask some additional questions about their education or training status in a moment.

Let me first clarify the situation with Blake - what's the arrangement you have or would like to have regarding the Child Benefit claim for Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Blake, their other parent lives with Blake and is already claiming the Child Benefit. I'm fine with that arrangement - they're the ones who have Blake living with them day-to-day, so it makes sense for them to get it.

For Alex, since no one else is claiming, I'd like to claim for Alex given that I'm contributing towards their costs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's very clear, thank you! 

**For Blake:** Since Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit and you're happy with that arrangement, you would **not be eligible to also claim for Blake**. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child, and in this case, the other parent has that claim.

**For Alex:** Since no one else is claiming for Alex and you're contributing towards their upkeep, we can continue with the eligibility assessment for Alex.

Let me now focus on **Alex's situation**. Since Alex is 16 years old, there are additional requirements to check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (16 years old), I need to know:**

**Is Alex currently in education or training, OR have they recently left education?**

For 16-19 year olds, Child Benefit can continue if they are:
- Currently enrolled in approved education or training, OR
- Have recently left education (within the last 20 weeks) and may qualify for an extension

**Which of these applies to Alex:**
1. Currently in education or training
2. Recently left education (within the last 20 weeks)
3. Neither - not in education and didn't recently leave

What is Alex's current situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is currently in education - they're studying for their A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is studying for A-levels, which is an approved form of education. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! A-levels are an approved qualification for Child Benefit. Now I need to check a few more details about Alex's education:

**1. Is Alex's A-level course full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)

**2. Is this course funded or arranged by Alex's employer, or does Alex have an agreement for a future job with an employer who's funding it?**

**3. If Alex is married or in a civil partnership**, are they financially dependent on you? (If Alex isn't married/in a civil partnership, you can skip this question)

Please let me know about these points.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
1. Yes, Alex's A-level course is full-time - it's definitely more than 12 hours per week.

2. No, it's not funded by an employer or anything like that - it's just regular sixth form education.

3. Alex isn't married or in a civil partnership.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex's A-level course meets all the requirements - it's full-time, non-advanced education that isn't employer-funded, and Alex isn't married. This is approved education for Child Benefit purposes.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about Alex's work situation:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

If Alex has a part-time job alongside their A-levels, that's fine as long as it's under 24 hours per week. If they're working 24 hours or more per week, that would disqualify them.

What's Alex's work situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work full-time - they're not working 24 hours a week or anything like that. They're focused on their A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is not working 24+ hours per week, so that's fine.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question about Alex:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right?** (Such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own name)

This would be benefits claimed by Alex themselves, not benefits you receive for them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex in an apprenticeship program?**

You mentioned Alex is doing A-levels, so I assume they're not in an apprenticeship, but I need to confirm this as part of the assessment.

(Note: Most apprenticeships would disqualify them, except for Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales which are approved)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship - they're just doing A-levels at sixth form.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex is doing A-levels at sixth form (not an apprenticeship), that's fine.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Good! We've already established that you live in the UK (in Manchester), so you meet the residency requirements for Alex as well.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: We've already confirmed that you contribute £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which meets the responsibility requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: We've already confirmed that no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, so let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about Alex's living situation:

**Where is Alex living?**

- In hospital (for an extended period)
- In local authority care
- With foster parents
- Normal living situation (with their other parent, as you mentioned)

Based on what you told me earlier, Alex lives with their other parent. Is that a normal living situation, or are there any special circumstances like fostering or care arrangements?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex just lives normally with their other parent - there's no fostering or care arrangements or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is living in a normal situation with their other parent.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Final question about Alex:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the council contributing financially towards where Alex lives or their upkeep? (This would generally prevent you from claiming Child Benefit)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex - it's just their other parent looking after them, and I help out with the £50 a week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's all the information I need. Let me now provide you with the final assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Final Decision**

Thank you for providing all that information. Here's my assessment for both of your children:

---

### **Blake (5 years old): NOT ELIGIBLE ❌**

**Why:** Blake is 5 years old and meets all the basic eligibility criteria (not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship, you meet residency requirements and live with Blake). However, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you are happy with this arrangement. 

**The key issue:** Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. Since Blake's other parent has an active claim and you're content with that arrangement, you cannot also claim for Blake.

---

### **Alex (16 years old): ELIGIBLE ✅**

**Why:** Alex meets all the eligibility requirements:
- ✅ Alex is 16 and in full-time approved education (A-levels - non-advanced education)
- ✅ Alex is not working 24+ hours per week
- ✅ Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✅ Alex is not in an apprenticeship
- ✅ You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester)
- ✅ You meet the responsibility requirement by contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit amount of £25.60/week)
- ✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- ✅ Alex lives in a normal situation with their other parent
- ✅ The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance

**You ARE eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex!**

---

## **Next Steps for Alex:**

1. **Apply for Child Benefit:**
   - Apply online at: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim
   - Or by post using form CH2

2. **Documents you'll need:**
   - Proof of your identity
   - Proof of Alex's identity
   - Alex's birth certificate or adoption certificate

3. **Payment details:**
   - Child Benefit is usually paid every 4 weeks
   - For Alex (as your only eligible child for this claim), you would receive **£25.60 per week** (approximately £111 per month)

4. **Important to know:**
   - **High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earn over £60,000, you may have to pay a tax charge. If either of you earns £80,000 or more, the charge equals the full Child Benefit amount (though you can still claim and opt not to receive payments to maintain National Insurance credits)

---

**Summary:** You can claim Child Benefit for Alex, but not for Blake (as their other parent already claims for them).

Do you have any questions about claiming for Alex?

reasonings: Blake is 5 years old and meets all the basic eligibility criteria (not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship, you meet residency requirements and live with Blake). However, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you are happy with this arrangement. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time, you are NOT eligible to claim for Blake while their other parent has an active claim.

Alex is 16 years old and in full-time approved education (A-levels, which is non-advanced education). Alex is not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), and you meet the responsibility requirement by contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit amount). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex lives in a normal situation with their other parent, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Therefore, you ARE ELIGIBLE to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Blake (5) and Alex (16). For Blake, although all eligibility criteria would be met, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit and the claimant is content with this arrangement. Since only one person can claim per child, the claimant cannot claim for Blake. For Alex, all eligibility requirements are satisfied: Alex is in approved education (A-levels), the claimant contributes adequately to Alex's upkeep, no one else is claiming, and all other criteria are met. The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex only.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Payments cover the child's upkeep
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Blake is 5 years old and meets all the basic eligibility criteria (not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship, you meet residency requirements and live with Blake). However, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you are happy with this arrangement. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time, you are NOT eligible to claim for Blake while their other parent has an active claim.

Alex is 16 years old and in full-time approved education (A-levels, which is non-advanced education). Alex is not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), and you meet the responsibility requirement by contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit amount). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex lives in a normal situation with their other parent, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Therefore, you ARE ELIGIBLE to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Blake (5) and Alex (16). For Blake, although all eligibility criteria would be met, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit and the claimant is content with this arrangement. Since only one person can claim per child, the claimant cannot claim for Blake. For Alex, all eligibility requirements are satisfied: Alex is in approved education (A-levels), the claimant contributes adequately to Alex's upkeep, no one else is claiming, and all other criteria are met. The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex only.
